item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A reasonably compelling set-up and some interesting characterization gets dragged down by an interview log that mostly conveys information that could in-universe have been conveyed through the description in about a tenth of the space. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Show… and tell, tell, tell, tell a lot, a whole lot, really, just keep telling" seems to be the motto here. You explain a lot, up to a point where everything loses its charm and just becomes a chore to read. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's correct as it is. They're asking what it is that is gone, not whether something they already knew about is the thing that is gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Muirfield: Hang on, now that what is gone?

Do you mean, "Hang on, now that's what's gone?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think there's a really good article here, but it's a shorter one. The interview in particular is way too long. The ending is gold, the rest not so much. The aside doesn't fit with the general SCP style: it's written as though it was part of a story. (Which it is, of course, but not in-universe.) You might consider breaking the interview into multiple sessions, but definitely find some other way to communicate the agents' discussion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That, and they just seem to take the PoI's word on it that he can control it.

he

They they they they they.1 It is the only pronoun by which the POI is referred to in any of the article. (Welcome to why I constantly argue that representation of non-cis people isn't meaningless in fiction.) <_<

Now, with that out of the way, the Foundation aren't just taking their word for it that they can control it; they're witnessing it happen that the two get along well:

SCP-2648 is notably not hostile toward POI-2648, and POI-2648 did not previously appear to notice that SCP-2648 is animate; this was elaborated upon in the attached interview transcript.

…unless you mean this:

When it was proposed by MTF Lambda-14 operatives that this event was likely a joint escape attempt between SCP-2648 and POI-2648, POI-2648 was informed of this suspicion and responded that such a suggestion was absurd and did not reflect the type of interactions they have with SCP-2648. POI-2648 instead cited that SCP-2648 was simply not aware of its growing size and did not realize it held the capacity for destruction.

If that's the case, then… this part still says that POI claims they did not intend to escape together, which would imply that they are claiming they can't control it, not the other way around.

Footnotes
1. I want a dollar for every 'he' in this discussion thread, and a shipping & handling charge of USD $59.99.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That, and they just seem to take the PoI's word on it that he can control it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's a lesson in anomalies not making sense, even in contexts other than physical and scientific.

Oh, okay. Yeah, this one definitely was weird and nonsensical, which is my favorite type of anomaly. I'd upgrade my upvote to a cherished "heck yea" upvote but alas wikidot has no option for heck yea upvores.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a situation that the Foundation is ill-equipped to handle, yet must deal with in some way. That aspect came through clearly.

The POI is an interesting character. I enjoyed the depiction, especially the tension between what they want (to be left alone with Friend) and what the Foundation wants (containment). My impression is that the POI is altered so frequently and so thoroughly that the altered state is the usual state; the description of losing the ability to 'see' was both clear and poignant.

How to handle an anomaly when you can't just stuff it in a metal box and keep an eye on it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I get off the psychs, my vision will fail again

This would seem to imply that they are on psychedelics a lot. Otherwise theyd be "blind" most of the time. Most of my problems with the article are based on this assertion. If this isn't the case, you should clarify because that's the interpretation I got from the piece. The agender references are clear upon rereading, I must've accidentally skipped over them. But the frequency of drug usage remains vague.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it seems really out of character for the foundation to allow everything happening here.

I'd definitely be interested in hearing specifics on this, if you're so inclined. I was thinking that maybe the Foundation wouldn't be too keen on open communication with police and the DEA while I was writing it, but I ended up letting it stay. Is it that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
like how the POI could constantly be on mushrooms and still speak like he's mostly sober

They, not he (the fact that they're not cis is literally all over the article and the author post, and is integral to the character) and also, we don't actually know how frequently they're eating shrooms, we just know it's relatively consistently. And trust me, you can eat a standard few grams of shrooms and act fairly normal. (Basic research will tell you this; I'm not attempting to, like, endorse eating shrooms in a forum post, lol.) LSD and the others, sure, but, again, we don't know how often they're consuming all of these (if you want to complain that I should have specified that and didn't, that'd be worth noting, and I'll consider editing something in for this reason1) we just know that it's regular. Given that they are able to function fairly normally, we can thus logically assume that it's not an absurd volume. There's an entire paragraph in the interview outlining and clarifying these aspects, too.

Other than those specifics, the rest of what you're saying is of course valid feedback, and I can understand the interpretation of it being a little contextless.

Footnotes
1. I did add something, as per what we were talking about in chat just now. Thank you for pointing this bit out

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the interview logs enough for an upvote, but it seems really out of character for the foundation to allow everything happening here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't really go anywhere. There are some smaller issues that make it seem like the author is unfamiliar with the subject matter, like how the POI could constantly be on mushrooms and still speak like he's mostly sober. The inclusion of LSD and DMT feel especially random and not particularly realistic. I feel like there's a lot of wasted potential here. The core concept is fine but the execution is lacking. A stronger narrative focus would benefit this article greatly, I think. Simply relying on weirdness doesn't cut it when psychedelics are so weird on their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I laughed at "multiple pineapples" and

POI-2648: What? You let it eat my shrooms? Come on, man, those are expensive!

Otherwise, an intriguing anomaly and a good story alongside it. It's just so frustrating, in-universe, because there's no real way to figure out what's going on here, or reason with either of these two, when it seems like reasoning is possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly have no idea what I was going for. I gave it a "does this at least read as coherent in terms of content and SCP formatting" check-through, and then I was like "K, not even gonna analyze the rest of what I just wrote."

Usually I like writing things that are huge deals with great threats present, like SCP-2758, because I'm a drama llama. I guess this is one of the times I was seeing what would happen if I wrote a skip that was, like, chill. And not a huge threat and shit. I don't even know.

edit:

so frustrating, in-universe, because there's no real way to figure out what's going on here, or reason with either of these two

okay so TL333's gave me the words I was looking for. Yeah. These two are tied, the Foundation have no idea what the fuck is going on, but when they try to do anything other than let them live on a plot of land and be high all the time, everything goes to hell and people get hurt. It's a lesson in the Foundation needing to mind their own business and it's a lesson in anomalies not making sense, even in contexts other than physical and scientific.

I guess that's what I was going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the fuck.

Upvoted because this is weird and I like weird things, but the story only invokes "wtf" and I dunno if that's the only emotion you were going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gonna say the main reason why I no-voted:

Meh.

It's well written enough, I just didn't feel for it.

item.title: author post
item.content:encoded:
Somewhere in South End when you were fun
you took my hand and you made me run
up past the prison to the seafront.
You climbed the cliff edge and took the plunge.
Why can’t we laugh now like we did then?
How come I see you and ache instead?

I had this idea at random today thanks to that song. So, I'm paying tribute to my home state of North Carolina with a coldpost about a shrooms-eating, acid-tabbing, DMT-huffing agender1 redneck with self-actualization issues. Oh, and their friend, who is a ball of flesh and dirt.

Photograph taken by me.

Footnotes
1. ATTENTION MY FRIENDS THERE SEEMS TO BE SOME CONFUSION ON THE FACT THAT THIS PERSON IS REFERRED TO AS 'THEY' IN THE ENTIRE ARTICLE AND EVEN MAKES A POINT OF CORRECTING THE INTERVIEWER ON 'SIR' IN ORDER TO REINFORCE THIS FACT
